items.title: Jump to the start of a line in the terminal
items.excerpt: Ctrl+A jumps your cursor to the beginning of the line — far faster than holding the left arrow.
items.body:
Hitting the left arrow to get back to the start of a long command is a waste of keystrokes.

Instead:

- **Ctrl+A** — jump to the **start** of the line
- **Ctrl+E** — jump to the **end** of the line

These are readline shortcuts, so they work in bash, zsh, and most REPLs.

snippets.label: Ctrl+A
snippets.detail: Move cursor to start of line

snippets.label: Ctrl+E
snippets.detail: Move cursor to end of line

items.title: What “idempotent” actually means
items.excerpt: An operation is idempotent if running it many times has the same effect as running it once.
items.body:
You'll see **idempotent** all over infra and automation docs.

It just means: running the thing repeatedly lands you in the same state as running it once. `mkdir -p foo` is idempotent — run it ten times, you still just have one `foo`. A plain `mkdir foo` is **not**: the second run errors.

This is why good automation aims to be idempotent — you can re-run it safely.

items.title: Docker won't free disk space until you prune
items.excerpt: Stopped containers, old images, and dangling volumes keep eating disk until you explicitly prune them.
items.body:
If your VPS is mysteriously running out of disk, Docker is a prime suspect.

Stopped containers and old image layers stick around. Reclaim space with:

```bash
docker system prune -a --volumes
```

Be careful: `--volumes` also removes unused volumes, so make sure nothing you need is only living in a dangling volume.
